channel.title: Electric Guitar Build 2025-2026 on DAEBFCG
channel.description: Recent content in Electric Guitar Build 2025-2026 on DAEBFCG

item.title: Sound Test - With Audio
item.description:
Sound Test

Jump down to the recordings…

Circuit Test Rig

For testing I attached JST-SM connectors to the pickup leads, as well as one from the string-ground/shielding wire. These make convenient and stable test connections - much better than alligator clips.

Recall that these pickup have three wires:

  • full output
  • tapped output
  • ground

Here is a photo of the test rig. It looks like a big mess, and it kind of is.

From the bottom right, and moving counter-clockwise:

1. Input switch box (copper colored square with the white breadboard on top)

The pickups go directly into this. It lets me switch each pickup between the full and tapped output, and also has a three-way switch to select each pickup individually or combine both in series.

2. Neck and Middle Tone (white box, red breadboard)

This consists of a rotary switch for selecting different capacitors and a pot for the amount applied.

This is coming out of the series connection and affects the neck pickup as a tone control, acts like a mid-cut when the pickups are together, and has no effect on the bridge pickup alone. It seems pretty useful in that I can have the neck pickup with a lot of tone applied but then switch to the middle or bridge position and still have a bright sound.

I currently have a .005, .015 and a .022 capacitor in this, and I think I like the .022 here.

1 MG pot, too, so when it is on 10 it has virtually no attenuation.

3. Main output module (large breadboard)

This has the output jack and various pots and such that I am using for bass-cut tone control and the master volume control (the last two components in the circuit).

Volume is a 500k linear pot.

Bass cut is a 1 MG pot with an 1100 picofarad capacitor in parallel with the signal. I tried a few cap values and this one seemed the best.

4. Tone Control Tester (black box)

This is another tone tester with a rotary switch and pot. I am using it for the master tone control, which affects all pickup combinations.

Caps are .015 and .022. I am leaning towards the .015 in this case. I also tried a .044 and as expected did not like the amount of mid range it removed.

500K audio pot.

5. Direct Out Switch (blue breadboard)

This comes next in line after the pickup selector, and allows completely bypassing all the volume and tone controls so the pickups go directly to the jack. I really like this option since it gives such a pure, bright, and LOUD sound at the flick of a switch.

Actually, that neck/middle tone stays in the circuit since it comes in parallel with this switch, so the real direct-out only happens when the bridge pickup is selected by itself, but that is ok.

Sound Samples

Here are some recordings of me noodling around. In each case, I started with the pickup(s) direct out, and then applied various combinations of volume, tone, and tap switching.

Neck Pickup

Pickups Together

Bridge Pickup

These were recorded with no amp, directly into an Audient ID4 audio interface into Ardour, with no signal processing applied at all.

The “reverb” you might hear is just the strings themselves resonating sympathetically which is how I tend to play.

The electric buzzing / static noises are due to all the unshielded wires in the test rig, and should not be present in the completed guitar.

I will record some more tests through an amp soon. And maybe play some actual tunes.

item.title: Pickups Installed
item.description:
Logo removal

I decided to remove the white “Seymour Duncan” logo from the pickups since I do not like any branding on the guitar. Internet lore suggested Magic-Eraser, acetone, alcohol, etc. None of these worked and I had to resort to scraping. This did not turn out as cleanly as I had hoped. It looks fine from six feet away but up close it is apparent that the logo was removed. I am thinking about painting the tops of these with matt black chalkboard paint, but that is a project for another day.

Pickups Installed

Installation was rather tedious. They are held in tight with some rubber tubing but I need to revisit the screw-hole strategy. I was planning to use some small nuts, CA glued into the bottom of the pickup routes, with a hole slightly wider than the screw into the body. I could not quite get this going, and just screwed directly into the wood. I need to rethink this and do it over, but it can be done later as well. Good enough for now.

Next up - sound test!

item.title: Nut and First String Up
item.description:
Nut

Some preliminary work was done on the nut earlier. I am using a dyed black bone nut from StewMac. (I have in the past cut my own from bovine dog-chew bone from the grocery store.)

First sanded to width.

Then the length.

Lastly, the slots are cut and it is shaped up and polished.

First Stringing

The strings are on for the first time! DAddario XL110 for now. These will come off and on a few times during setup and will be replaced with a nicer Pyramid set in the end.

First Impressions: Plays nice and is quite bright (as expected). I played it like this for a couple hours to let it settle and to get a sense of things. Chords sound especially good - very clear. Sound is balanced but definitely leans to the high-end side. Single notes seem fine but will need to be evaluated through pickups and amp to really know.

item.title: Frets Level and Crown
item.description:
Leveling the Frets

The goal is to get all the frets to the exact same height. Mostly they are, but there are some variations due to different wood density along the fretboard and varying installation pressure.

This is crucial to avoid string buzz, misfretting and other playability issues.

With the truss rod adjusted so the fretboard is perfectly flat, a file is run over them to remove any high spots. Each fret has a line of Sharpie marker across it to identify which frets are getting hit. Once all the marker lines are rubbed off they are all the same height.

Fortunately there is not much work needed since I was careful during fret installation.

Then each fret is checked with a rocker (card scraper) to check its height relative to its two neighbors. If it rocks, it is high.

Any high spots are fine tuned with a small file.

Crowning

Now the frets are all the same height, but flat on top. Time to recreate the rounded top (crown) across them.

This is done with a concave-shaped crowning file (medium width burr).

Polish

Once the crowns are reestablished it is time to sand out all the file marks and polish up the frets. I start with 320 grit. Then down to 600, 1,000, and all the way to 12,000. 1,500 through 12,000 are done with micro mesh polishing pads.

Finally I go over them with a buffing wheel on the dremel.

This process gets them to a mirror like finish. The photo does not do them justice, but they are really slick and should play nicely.

The black residue from the metal polishing will come right off with some naptha.

Stainless Steel Frets

This is my first time using stainless steel frets. I was a bit worried since they are much harder than standard nickel-silver and allegedly much more difficult to work and rough on the tools. I did not really find this to be the case. They are harder and take a few more file strokes and sanding passes to remove material, but it is not a big deal at all.

Supposedly they do not wear out.

I like them.

item.title: Fretting
item.description:
Fret Preparation

First we make each fret.

This consists of:

  1. Bending to the correct radius
  2. Cutting each fret to the correct length
  3. Removing the end of the tang that overlaps the fretboard binding
  4. Pre-dressing the ends

I am using pre-bent fretwire, so step 1 can be skipped. I have used fretwire from 2 foot straight lengths as well as long coiled pieces, and getting it pre-radiused is the way to go. This is from Philadelphia Luthier Tools who sells packs of 25 pre-bent to a 12 inch radius for close to the same cost as buying in bulk.

This guitar is getting 47x104 Jescar stainless steel.

Cut to length and tang removal

This is easily done with a Dremel and metal-cutting disk.

Each fret is cut to length and de-tanged. Some light filing on the bottom where the tang was removed is usually needed.

The prepared frets are kept organized in a holder.

End Dressing

I pre-dress the ends of my frets before installing.

First I pre-file each corner to a 45 degree angle, then come in over the top with the fret-crowning file also at a 45 degree angle. This is just to give the next step a head-start.

Then the semi-hemispherical shape is ground smooth on a special emery polishing wheel in the Dremel. This concave-edged wheel was made by running it over a small round file.

Just a couple seconds in this and the fret ends come out remarkably round and smooth with no rough edges at all.

Installation

With the frets prepared to this extent, installation is a breeze.

First a bead of hide glue is spread into the fret slot. Then wiped off flush with DRY paper towel.

(Notice how I accidentally skipped a fret trying to get a photo of the process…)

The fret is then positioned and lightly pressed (not pounded) in with a soft mallet just enough to get it to stay put.

The fret is seated home using a fret-press caul mounted in the drill press.

Lastly, the excess glue is wiped off with a warm wet rag.

Fretboard finishing

I let the hide glue dry about four hours and lightly sand the fretboard between the frets to 600 (to knock back the raised grain from being wetted) , then get the first coat of tung oil on the fretboard. This stuff takes several days to cure between coats.

The first coat is a 50/50 mix of pure tung oil and food-grade D-limonene. The thinning this way helps the first coat penetrate much better (and dry faster). The second coat will be straight tung oil.

Before:

After:

At this stage, the frets are sort of rough looking from the shaping and installation process. These will shine up nicely once the leveling and crowning is done later.

item.title: Fittings
item.description:
Truss Rod Cover

A small cover plate goes over the truss-rod adjustment port.

This is made from a headstock offcut (so that it blends in visually with the neck laminations) with a slice of cherry glued to the back of it laid horizontally for strength. Overall it is about 3/32 thick.

The two screws land at the 24 fret position.

String Ferrules

Black string-through ferrules from Philadelphia Luthier Tools, made of solid brass.

The holes in the back of the guitar need to be drilled out to 3/8 wide by 3/8 deep. The drill press pin-from-the-other-side technique is not working for me since the 3/8 brad point bit is so long it wanders due to runout on the drill press. To get the holes centered correctly I had to plug the existing 1/8 holes and reestablish the center points.

Then carefully make them 3/8 deep so the ferrules are flush with the back.

They are a tight fit and need to be tapped in with a hammer and wooden dowel.

I will remove them before finishing, and reinstall again afterwards, possibly gluing in with a drop of CA in each.

Only a slight amount of tear-out on the third string hole in the walnut which had to be sanded out. Otherwise, not too bad.

Control Cavity Cover

I was originally planning to make this out of a sheet of 12 gauge brass. Looking around for something to use as a material for a temporary cover, I found something that works and is good enough for the real thing.

Someone might get a smile someday when they open this up to work on it.

Polished up it looks pretty good.

Back fittings are done!

item.title: Pre Finish
item.description:
Now the entire guitar is sanded end to end.

First, 150 grit is used to remove any remaining carving marks and to fix any remaining contour and shaping issues.

This is followed by a thorough going-over with 220 grit.

All scratches must be eliminated.

Then another round of 360.

Finally a light pass with 600 and 1000 grits. Possibly overkill, but I like the result.

Now one seal-coat of 1-pound-cut platina shellac is applied.

Looking pretty decent.

This seal coat will be kept on for the remainder of the build, the fret installation, and the test-playing period when the electronics circuit is finalized. It will be again sanded to 1000 grit before the final French Polish shellac finish is applied.

item.title: Bridge Mounting
item.description:
We will be installing 6 of these.

Determine Intonation Points

The first thing to do is determine the lengthwise positions for the bridges. This will be done for the two outermost strings, and the inner four strings will be put in a straight connecting line between them. I am not going to directly use the measured scale lengths (25.5" low E, and 25" high E), but instead determine the positions empirically so as to include the additional length needed to account for string stretching, etc. (i.e. “compensation”). The bridge saddles are adjustable for fine tuning the intonation, which is good, but I would like to try to get them close so minimal adjustment is needed.

I set up an arrangement where the two outer e strings are attached to tuners, with a temporary nut and frets at the 0 and 12 position. The bottom end of the strings are attached to a jig I made for this operation that acts as a tailpiece to hold the strings. The idea is that I can compare the 12th fret harmonic with the 12th fret fretted note and move the bridge forward or backward until the pitches are equal. That will give the the intonation point for the string, and thus determine where the bridge should be mounted.

Once this is done for the high and low e strings, a line is drawn from point to point, which should make about a 13-14 degree angle.

I first tried to use the actual bridges, but the tail end of them is too high to fit right.

I switched to a small triangular block of wood, which works much better, and is easier to mark the intonation point.

First Attempt

My first method for this was to get the two outermost bridges screwed in, and then lay out the remaining four in a line and screw them in. Unfortunately, the planned distance between the outermost strings on this guitar (2.0625") is a bit more than the minimal width these bridges are designed for so there will be some gap between them. So I cannot just lay them in side to side touching each other.

The ideal side to side placement in this case is much like the principle used for string spacing on a nut - the thicker strings get a slightly larger gap between them, so overall the strings come out equally spaced.

I tried calculating the positions taking into account this gap, but it worked out to about 1/100th of an inch extra between the low e, a, and d strings, and 0 gap betwen the treble strings.

I took a shot at this, basically eyeballing the gaps and drilled the 3/32 holes for the screws.

Here is how that worked out.

It is not terrible, but far from perfect. The worst part is that the d string bridge is too low, which throws off the line and sort of stands out as not right. Also the gaps are not nearly what I had hoped.

This is all a result of the way I drilled the screw holes.

Looking at the holes, it is clear there is some misalignment.

I decided that it is not acceptable, and will need to try again.

ReDo

This time I worked out exactly where all the holes should go on the computer and printed out a template. The template has cross-lines whereever a hole should go, and I used a fine pointed scratch awl to pinprick those locations through the paper and into the wood.

First I have to fill in the holes from the first attempt, though. I made 6 little 3/32 dowels from cherry and CA glued them in, and cut flush.

This technique of twisting some wood through a hole in metal works amazingly well for making small dowels.

The holes are nicely plugged now, giving a clean slate for another try. This will all be covered by the bridges, so no one will ever see these. (Fortunately I only drilled the top hole for each bridge.)

Template

Here is the template.

Ideally, this would be 3D printed or made by CNC with the holes premade.

A test placement looks good.

The next improvement to my process was to start by drilling very small pilot holes, like 3/64 inch, then working up to 5/64 and then 3/32 incrementally, instead of going right in with the 3/32 bit.

First impressions:

The alignment looks much better. The gapping is still not perfect.

This will have to be good enough.

(Also visible are some remnants of wax I used on the screws to make them easier to screw in.)

String Holes

Now for the string holes. These bridges can support either top-load or string-through-body, so I am going to set up for both options. Again using a pin prick awl, I made the holes along a the line from the outermost strings, and drilled the 1/8 holes with a brad point bit.

Then used a 5/16 brad point to make the recess for the top-loading option.

The 1/8 holes need to go all the way through the body for the string-through-body option. For that I used the pin trick to get the drill bit exactly aligned from the top hole so I could complete the hole from the back.

String through holes from the back.

Some metal string ferrules will be mounted in the back of the guitar where these 1/8 holes are now. The ferrules have not arrived yet, so I will wait to drill out the recesses for those.

These look to be in good alignment.

item.title: Fretboard Radius
item.description:
The guitar is positioned and supported so that the neck remains flat and does not flex during the sanding process. Some protective paper is used to cover the body and headstock.

The neck support block used here is a rolling-pin holder I picked up from a flea market for $3. StewMac sells the same thing, lined with cork, for over $20.

A StewMac 12 inch radius sanding block is used. (A longer radius beam would be ideal.) A section of 80 grit sanding-belt is stuck to the concave surface with double-sided tape.

The important thing is to ensure that the fretboard surface remains flat and of equal thickness all around during sanding.

This is followed up with 220 grit. I will take another few passes with 400-grit before installing the frets.

The fretboard now has a nice 12 inch radius across the top.

Fortunately any overly deep dot inlays have now come to the surface, and the slotting-mishap scratch at the 20th fret has completely disappeared.

The side dots are also centered now, so my calculations were correct in placing them slightly below center.

A knotty spot around the 8th and 9th frets has been revealed, and the wood there seems hard yet more brittle than typical walnut. I think this will be ok, and hopefully made durable with the application of Tung oil. I am a little worried though. Will the guitar need a fretboard replacement someday?

item.title: Neck Carving
item.description:
The general procedure is simple - remove everything that does not look like a neck.

These photos are slightly out of order and organized roughly into distinct steps, but in reality the whole neck process is one fluid activity with lots of iteration.

Holding Jig

A special cruciform holding jig that can be firmly held in the vise is made to support the guitar for neck carving.

Some additional 1/4 mdf and padding is added to the back to prop up the body since the fretboard is already attached.

The guitar firmly held face down to the cruciform with three cam clamps.

Taper

The sides of the (currently square) neck are tapered to match the fretboard using a block plane.

On a neck that is being made unattached to the body, the bandsaw is better suited. In this case, the headstock and body are in the way.

Thickness

The target final thickness of the neck is approximately 3/4 inch. It is currently 1 - 1.5 inch thick (since it extends into the body), so a significant amount of wood will need to be removed.

I made some cross cuts to the depth and sawed the out the excess with the Ryoba saw, then cleaned up with a chisel.

To be sure I did not cut too deep, I set up a guide rail using some aluminum angle bar. For future reference, leave an additional 1/8 inch thickness.

This is also usually done on the bandsaw, but not feasible with the body attached.

Heel

To start the neck shaping I will focus on the neck-to-body transition (i.e. the heel) first.

The plan is to create a smooth transition under the fretting hand, with the curve based on the multi-scale fret angles.

Headstock Transition

Next the neck-to-headstock transition is worked on.

Shaping

With the headstock and heel areas roughed out, the overall shaping can be done.

The first tool using is the Shinto rasp, which removes a lot of wood quickly get the initial facets cut.

This is followed up with a four-in-hand rasp to further whittle the neck into the rounded shape.

The edges where it meets the fretboard are cut down with a chisel.

The thickness is checked along the way.

Starting to look like a neck.

Heel Refinement

With the neck closer to its final shape, the heel shape is refined.

A test fit with the fretting hand is done to be sure it feels right.

The body cutaway curves are also refined into their final contour and roundover.

Headstock Refinement

The same refinement is also done at the headstock end.

Now the fretboard-to-headstock transition is cleaned up.

Sanding

To finish up, the entire neck is sanded with 80 and 100 grit paper to remove all rasp marks and perform the last bit of shaping work.

Finally, it is sanded all over with 150 grit to remove any scratches.

The cherry wood has some odd dark spots and pitch-pockets. The crescent shape near the headstock is not a carving mistake, but a dark spot in the wood.
